title: Music Interval Calculator - Note Distances
tagline: Find interval names and semitone distances between two musical notes in seconds.
intro: Enter any two chromatic notes to calculate the ascending interval name and the descending semitone distance.
formula: Logic: normalize both notes, convert them to chromatic indexes, then measure the ascending and descending semitone distances modulo 12.

error.invalidNote: Enter two valid chromatic notes such as C, F#, Bb, or Eb.
error.invalidOctave: Enter a whole-number octave or leave the octave field blank.

result.heading: Result
result.octaveAssumption: Octave note: assuming octave {value} for both notes.

about.heading: About the music interval calculator
about.body:
Intervals are the building blocks of melody, harmony, ear training, arranging, and improvisation. Every time you hear two notes and describe their relationship as a major third, perfect fifth, tritone, or octave, you are naming an interval. This calculator helps translate note names into that theoretical language instantly, making it useful for students, producers, instrumentalists, singers, and anyone trying to connect the keyboard or fretboard to music theory vocabulary.

The calculator works from the chromatic scale. Each note maps to a semitone index from zero to eleven, and the distance between two note names is measured by counting semitone steps upward. That ascending distance determines the interval name used here. For example, moving from C to G covers seven semitones, which is the familiar perfect fifth. Moving from D# to A covers six semitones, giving the unstable, dramatic tritone that appears so often in tension-heavy harmony.

Enharmonic spelling matters in musical notation, but for quick semitone calculation many spellings share the same pitch class. Db and C# land on the same chromatic step, Eb and D# do the same, and so on. This tool normalizes those enharmonic spellings so you can type common flats or sharps and still receive an immediate answer. That makes it practical for ear-training drills, songwriting sessions, rehearsal discussions, or lessons where people may spell the same sound in different ways.

The ascending and descending distances are both useful. The ascending value tells you how far it takes to move upward from the first note to the second, while the descending value shows the reverse motion. In performance and arranging, direction changes the way a phrase feels even when the pitch classes are identical. A leap up a major sixth often sounds very different from moving down its complementary distance.

Use this calculator as a fast theory aid, especially when checking interval recognition, building chords, writing counterlines, or learning your instrument. It is intentionally focused on direct chromatic distance rather than full notation spelling rules, so it works best as an accessible pitch-class reference that keeps the core musical relationship clear.

examples.heading: Music interval examples
examples.intro: Three quick interval checks using common note pairs.

items.note: C up to G spans seven semitones, one of the most stable intervals in tonal music.
This six-semitone span is the classic tritone, famous for tension and color.
Ascending from F to E covers eleven semitones, producing a major seventh.

howto.heading: How to use the music interval calculator
steps: Type the first note using a natural, sharp, or flat spelling such as C, F#, or Bb.
Type the second note you want to compare against the first note.
Optionally enter an octave number if you want the page to display the shared-octave assumption.
Click Calculate to see the interval name plus the ascending and descending semitone distances.

faq.heading: Music interval calculator FAQ
faq.q1: Why does Bb return the same pitch class as A#?
faq.q1_a: They are enharmonic equivalents in twelve-tone equal temperament, so they map to the same chromatic index for this calculator.
faq.q2: Why does the calculator show ascending and descending distances separately?
faq.q2_a: Because direction matters musically. The upward motion from one note to another is not always described or felt the same way as the reverse motion.
faq.q3: Does this tool name compound intervals such as ninths or tenths?
faq.q3_a: No. It intentionally reports pitch-class distance inside a single octave, so the named result falls within unison through octave.
faq.q4: Is this useful for ear training?
faq.q4_a: Yes. Many musicians use interval drills to connect note pairs with interval names and semitone counts, and this tool gives instant feedback.
